Statistical Inference 1

Samantha Leorato

1. Review of classical testing theory: Fisher and Neyman-Pearson approaches.

Main references for this part:

  • (Casella and Berger 2024),
  • (Pace and Salvan 1997),
  • (Lehmann 1993),
  • (Lehmann and Romano 2022),
  • (Keener 2010)

What is a test: types of error, other definitions

Let us start with a quick review of what a test is. In the common view, an hypothesisis to be tested is defined relative of its complementary hypothesis: if the data suggest it is false, what should we believe to be true?

Def: The two complementary hypotheses are called null hypothesis (H0H0) and alternative hypothesis (H1H1)

The Null Hypothesis is the hypothesis that is held to be true unless sufficient evidence to the contrary is obtained

In hypothesis testing, the objective is to use data to decide if H0H0 is plausible, or if the evidence does not support it enough and we need to reject it (although it is ofter true that we are actually hoping to reject it).

The definition of a hypothesis test requires the definition of a model (parametric or not) and a specification of the null and alternative hypotheses as submodels.

Parametric model

For the moment, we consider the case of a parametric model: {f(⋅∣θ):θ∈Θ}{f(⋅∣θ):θ∈Θ} where θθ is a (possibly vector values) parameter, ΘΘ is the parameter space, f(⋅∣θ)f(⋅∣θ) is a family of distributions (continuous or discrete, univariate or not), indexed by θθ.

In this context, a hypothesis can take the form H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θ1,H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θ1, where typically (but not necessarily) Θ1=Θ∖Θ0Θ1=Θ∖Θ0 is the conjugate of the null parametric set.

Types of hypotheses

We can have simple and composite hypotheses:

  • A simple hypothesis determines completely the distribution of XX, specifies only one possible distribution for the sample (in a parametric framework, it specifies exactly the parameter value). (for example given a Gaussian model with σ2=1σ2=1, the hypothesis, H0:μ=0H0:μ=0)
  • a composite hypothesis specifies more that one possible distributions for the sample (for example, given a Gaussian model with σ2=1σ2=1, the hypothesis H0:μ≥2H0:μ≥2)

Rejection region

With this setting, we can define:

Def: A hypothesis procedure or hypothesis test is a rule that specifies:

  • For which sample values the decision is made to accept H0H0 as true
  • For which sample values H0H0 is rejected (and H1H1 is accepted as true)

The decision is based (typically) on the definition of a rejection region (or critical region) defined by the subset of the sample space that leads to reject the null hypothesis.

Rejection region

The rejection region RR of a hypothesis test is usually defined through a test statistic W(X)W(X), a function of the sample R={X:W(X)>c}⇒rejectH0R={X:W(X)>c}⇒rejectH0 A={X:W(X)≤c}⇒acceptH0A={X:W(X)≤c}⇒acceptH0 The definition of RR as a function of WW directly simplifies the definition of RR, because permits to write it as a subset of RR instead of RnRn
Rw={w:W>c}Rw={w:W>c} Once the region is defined, and a decision (reject or not) is taken, there is no guarantee that it is correct.

Errors

In fact, there are 4 possible outcomes of the decision problem, two of which correspond to wrong decisions: a type I error occurs if H0H0 is rejected when it is actually true; a type II error occurs if H0H0 is not rejected when it is actually false.

Decision H0 is true H1 is true
H0 Correct (1−α1−α) Type II error
H1 Type I error (αα) Correct

Types of errors, power function

Errors in hypothesis testing are not symmetric. Conventionally, the statistical testing procedures (both Fisher and Neyman-Pearson) tend to assign a greater importance to αα, the type I error. This is the error that is controlled for. This is the reason why the null hypothesis is (quite often) defined as the one whose incorrect rejection is considered more severe.

Unfortunately, the two error types enter into conflict: by reducing αα, the type II error (also called ββ) increases, and vice-versa.

Example: trade-off between αα and ββ

library(shiny)
library(dplyr)

To keep things simple, we assume the same Gaussian population X∼N(μ,σ2)X∼N(μ,σ2), σ2=1σ2=1 known and the following simple hypotheses:

H0:μ=0vsH1:μ=2H0:μ=0vsH1:μ=2

The test statistic used for this type of hypotheses is Z=¯Xσ/√nZ=X¯σ/n The rejection region is Z>t(n−1)1−α/2Z>t1−α/2(n−1).

Example: trade-off between αα and ββ

0.0010.25000.050.0010.0260.0510.0760.1010.1260.1510.1760.2010.2260.25

The type I and type II errors can’t be both minimized.

type I error type II error
error probabilities 0.050 0.361

Power function

We now need to recall some definitions

Def: A test procedure with rejection region RR, for the hypothesis H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θ1H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θ1 has power function β(θ)β(θ) defined as: β(θ)=Pr(X∈R∣θ)β(θ)=Pr(X∈R∣θ) for θ∈Θ0∪Θ1θ∈Θ0∪Θ1.

So, the power is the probability to reject the null hypothesis given a certain value of θθ.

Power function

Let X1,…,X10X1,…,X10 be a random sample from a population N(μ,σ2=4)N(μ,σ2=4) and let H0:μ≤0and H1:μ>0H0:μ≤0and H1:μ>0 and let consider a test with R={¯X>1}R={X¯>1} Then,

Level and size of a test

Def:

  • For 0≤α≤10≤α≤1, a test with power function β(θ)β(θ) is a size αα test if supθ∈Θ0β(θ)=αsupθ∈Θ0β(θ)=α
  • For 0≤α≤10≤α≤1, a test with power function β(θ)β(θ) is a level αα test if supθ∈Θ0β(θ)≤αsupθ∈Θ0β(θ)≤α

Neyman-Pearson theorem

Fisher advocated the use of a test statistic and look for evidence agaist the null through the significance level but was not so concerned about the definition of an optimality procedure that could be valid universally.

Neyman and Pearson proposed this resut because they wanted to give a mathematical justification to the tests proposed by Fisher’s procedure.

Since both type I and type II errors can’t be simultaneously minimized, Neyman and Pearson proposed to select the best test based on a constrained minimization problem: minimization of Type II error, given the constraint on a fixed Type I error (level) αα.

Uniformly most powerful tests

They elaborate the concept of Most powerful and Uniformly most powerful (UMP) tests.

Def: Let CC be a class of tests for testing H0:θ∈Θ0H0:θ∈Θ0 versus H1:θ∈Θ1H1:θ∈Θ1. A test belonging to a class CC (of possible tests), with power function β(θ)β(θ), is a uniformly most powerful (UMP) class CC test if, β(θ)≥β′(θ)β(θ)≥β′(θ) for every θ∈Θ1(=Θc0)θ∈Θ1(=Θ0c) and for every β′(θ)β′(θ) that is a power function of a test in class CC.

If the chosen family CC is the set of αα level tests, then the definition above gives the UMP level αα tests.

If Θ0={θ0}Θ0={θ0} and Θ1={θ1}Θ1={θ1} (simple hypotheses), then a test satisfying the definition above is said most powerful.

Randomized tests

For technical reasons, it is convenient to allow a randomization mechanism, to help with the decision between H0H0 and H1H1.

A randomized test is characterized by a critical function ϕϕ with range in [0,1][0,1]. Given xx, ϕ(x)ϕ(x) is the probability to reject H0H0.

In this case, the power function corresponds to the expectation of the critical function with respect to a given parameter: β(θ)=EθPθ{reject H0∣X}=Eθϕ(X)β(θ)=EθPθ{reject H0∣X}=Eθϕ(X) Note that the set of critical functions ΦΦ is convex: if ϕ1ϕ1 and ϕ2ϕ2 are critical function (belong to ΦΦ), then for any τ∈(0,1)τ∈(0,1), also τϕ1+(1−τ)ϕ2∈Φτϕ1+(1−τ)ϕ2∈Φ.

A nonrandomized test is a randomized test where R={x:ϕ(x)=1}R={x:ϕ(x)=1}.

Neyman-Pearson theorem

Consider testing H0:θ=θ0H0:θ=θ0 versus H1:θ=θ1H1:θ=θ1 where the pdf or pmf corresponding to θiθi is f(x∣θi),i=0,1f(x∣θi),i=0,1.

  1. There exist a test ϕϕ and a constant kk such that Eθ0ϕ(X)=αandEθ0ϕ(X)=αand ϕ(x)={1when f(x∣θ1)>kf(x∣θ0)0when f(x∣θ1)<kf(x∣θ0)ϕ(x)={1when f(x∣θ1)>kf(x∣θ0)0when f(x∣θ1)<kf(x∣θ0)
  2. If a test satisfies the above conditions for some kk, then it is most powerful for testing θ0θ0 against θ1θ1
  3. If ϕϕ is most powerful, then there exists kk such that ϕϕ is defined as above (a.e.) unless there exists a test with size <α<α and power 1.

The result

If ϕ∈{0,1}ϕ∈{0,1} (nonrandomized test), the Neyman-Pearson Lemma states that every most powerful level αα test, if it exists, must have the rejection region: R={x:f(x∣θ1)≥kf(x∣θ0)}R={x:f(x∣θ1)≥kf(x∣θ0)}

for some k>0k>0 for which Pθ0(X∈R)=αPθ0(X∈R)=α.

Also, if a test with rejection region RR as above exists and Pθ0(X∈R)=αPθ0(X∈R)=α exists, then the UMP α−α−level test is a size αα test.

Proof - 1

1- Let α(k)=Pθ0{f(x∣θ1)>kf(x∣θ0)}α(k)=Pθ0{f(x∣θ1)>kf(x∣θ0)}. Assume f(x∣θ0)>0f(x∣θ0)>0 (the probability is computed under H0H0). α(k)α(k) is the probability that the rv f(X∣θ1)/f(X∣θ0)>kf(X∣θ1)/f(X∣θ0)>k. Thus, 1−α(k)1−α(k) is a CDF (not necessarily continuous), with α(k−)−α(k)=Pθ0(f(X∣θ1)/f(X∣θ0)=k)α(k−)−α(k)=Pθ0(f(X∣θ1)/f(X∣θ0)=k).

Given α∈(0,1)α∈(0,1), let k0k0 be such that α(k0)≤α≤α(k−0)α(k0)≤α≤α(k0−) and define ϕ(x)=⎧⎪ ⎪ ⎪⎨⎪ ⎪ ⎪⎩1when f(x∣θ1)>k0f(x∣θ0)α−α(k0)α(k−0)−α(k0)when f(x∣θ1)=k0f(x∣θ0)0when f(x∣θ1)<k0f(x∣θ0)ϕ(x)={1when f(x∣θ1)>k0f(x∣θ0)α−α(k0)α(k0−)−α(k0)when f(x∣θ1)=k0f(x∣θ0)0when f(x∣θ1)<k0f(x∣θ0)

Proof - 2

If α(k−0)−α(k0)=0α(k0−)−α(k0)=0, then Pθ0{f(x∣θ1)=k0f(x∣θ0)}=0Pθ0{f(x∣θ1)=k0f(x∣θ0)}=0 and ϕϕ is defined a.e.

It is easy to see that the size of the test is Eθ0ϕ(X)=αEθ0ϕ(X)=α

2- Suppose that ϕϕ satisfies the definition of the theorem, and assume ϕ∗ϕ∗ is any other test with Eθ0ϕ∗≤αEθ0ϕ∗≤α.

Let S+={x:ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x)>0}andS−={ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x)<0}S+={x:ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x)>0}andS−={ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x)<0}

If x∈S+x∈S+, then necessarily ϕ(x)>0ϕ(x)>0 and thus f(x∣θ1)≥kf(x∣θ0)f(x∣θ1)≥kf(x∣θ0). If x∈S−x∈S−, then necessarily ϕ(x)<1ϕ(x)<1 and thus f(x∣θ1)≤kf(x∣θ0)f(x∣θ1)≤kf(x∣θ0).

Proof - 2

Thus, ∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))(f(x∣θ1)−kf(x∣θ0))dx=∫S+∪S−(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))(f(x∣θ1)−kf(x∣θ0))dx≥0∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))(f(x∣θ1)−kf(x∣θ0))dx=∫S+∪S−(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))(f(x∣θ1)−kf(x∣θ0))dx≥0 and this implies βϕ(θ1)−βϕ∗(θ1)=Eθ1ϕ(X)−Eθ1ϕ∗(X)=∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))f(x∣θ1)dx≥∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))kf(x∣θ0)dx≥0βϕ(θ1)−βϕ∗(θ1)=Eθ1ϕ(X)−Eθ1ϕ∗(X)=∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))f(x∣θ1)dx≥∫(ϕ(x)−ϕ∗(x))kf(x∣θ0)dx≥0

3- See (Lehmann and Romano 2022)

Example

Suppose X∼binomial(2,θ)X∼binomial(2,θ) and we are interested in testing H0:θ=1/2H0:θ=1/2 vs H1:θ=3/4H1:θ=3/4.

The likelihood ratio L(X)=f3/4(X)f1/2(X)=(2X)(3/4)X(1/4)2−X(2X)(1/2)2=3X4(under H0)=⎧⎪⎨⎪⎩1/4pr.1/43/4pr.1/29/4pr.1/4L(X)=f3/4(X)f1/2(X)=(2X)(3/4)X(1/4)2−X(2X)(1/2)2=3X4=(under H0){1/4pr.1/43/4pr.1/29/4pr.1/4

Assume α=0.05α=0.05 is the desired level.

We have Pθ0(L(X)≤3/4)=3/4<1−αPθ0(L(X)≤3/4)=3/4<1−α, thus k>3/4k>3/4, but if k<9/4k<9/4, then Pθ0(L(X)>k)=Pθ0(L(X)=9/4)=1/4>αPθ0(L(X)>k)=Pθ0(L(X)=9/4)=1/4>α, thus k≥9/4k≥9/4.

However, k>9/4k>9/4 is also impossible, because then Pθ0(L(X)>k)=0Pθ0(L(X)>k)=0 (implying ϕ=0ϕ=0).

Thus, we must have k=9/4k=9/4 and ϕ(0)=ϕ(1)=0ϕ(0)=ϕ(1)=0, while ϕ(2)ϕ(2) is determined by: α=Eθ0ϕ(X)=14ϕ(2)⇒ϕ(2)=4α.α=Eθ0ϕ(X)=14ϕ(2)⇒ϕ(2)=4α.

Corollary

From factorization theorem, it is easy to derive:

Corollary: Consider testing H0:θ=θ0H0:θ=θ0 versus H1:θ=θ1H1:θ=θ1. Suppose T(X)T(X) is a sufficient statistics for θθ and g(t|θi),i=0,1g(t|θi),i=0,1 is its prob. density function corresponding to θiθi. Then any test statistics based on TT with rejection region SS is a most powerful level αα test if it satisfies t=T(x)∈Sifg(t|θ1)>kg(t|θ0)t=T(x)∈Sifg(t|θ1)>kg(t|θ0) and t=T(x)∈SCifg(t|θ1)<kg(t|θ0)t=T(x)∈SCifg(t|θ1)<kg(t|θ0) and α=Pθ0(T∈S)α=Pθ0(T∈S)

Example

X∼exponential(λ)X∼exponential(λ) and H0:λ=1vsH1:λ=2H0:λ=1vsH1:λ=2 The rejection region must be defined as: R={x:e−∑ixi≥k2ne−2∑ixi}R={x:e−∑ixi≥k2ne−2∑ixi} where kk is such that P(X∈R∣λ=1)=P1(e−∑iXi≥k2ne−2∑iXi)=P1(e∑iXi≥k2n)=P1(∑iXi≥log(k)+nlog2)=αP(X∈R∣λ=1)=P1(e−∑iXi≥k2ne−2∑iXi)=P1(e∑iXi≥k2n)=P1(∑iXi≥log⁡(k)+nlog⁡2)=α

Example

Since ∑iXi∼Gamma(n,1)∑iXi∼Gamma(n,1), one can find kk.

Likelihood ratio test

According to N-P Lemma, test of the form: R={x:f(x∣θ1)f(x∣θ0)≥k}R={x:f(x∣θ1)f(x∣θ0)≥k} is an MP test of level α=Pθ0(X∈R)α=Pθ0(X∈R) (unless the event {f(x∣θ1)f(x∣θ0)=k}{f(x∣θ1)f(x∣θ0)=k} has positive probabilty, in that case randomization is necessary).

The region RR is defined as the ratio between the likelihood function under the alternative and the likelihood function under the null hypothesis.

This is very simple because NP Lemma only considers simple hypothesis (both for H0H0 and H1H1).

This suggests the definition of likelihood ratio tests for arbitrary testing problems.

Generalized likelihood ratio tests

Suppose we have H0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈ΘcoH0:θ∈Θ0vsH1:θ∈Θoc The values fof θθ under H0H0 and H1H1 are not unique

However, suppose that the MLE exists over the whole parameter space Θ=Θ0∪Θc0Θ=Θ0∪Θ0c

We can also obtain the MLE for θθ under the null hypothesis (through constrained optimization): ^θ0=argmaxθ∈Θ0ℓ(θ∣x)=argmaxθ∈Θ0logf(x∣θ)θ^0=arg⁡maxθ∈Θ0ℓ(θ∣x)=arg⁡maxθ∈Θ0log⁡f(x∣θ) Then, we take the ratio between the likelihood under H0H0, f(x∣^θ0)f(x∣θ^0) and the likelihood under the parameter space ΘΘ (that is the unconstrained MLE): ^θ=argmaxθ∈Θℓ(θ∣x)θ^=arg⁡maxθ∈Θℓ(θ∣x)

Generalized likelihood ratio tests

The (generalized) likelihood ratio test statistic is then: λ(x)=maxθ∈ΘL(θ∣x)maxθ∈Θ0L(θ∣x)=L(^θ∣x)L(^θ0∣x)=f(x∣^θ)f(x∣^θ0)λ(x)=maxθ∈ΘL(θ∣x)maxθ∈Θ0L(θ∣x)=L(θ^∣x)L(θ0^∣x)=f(x∣θ^)f(x∣θ^0)

  • In typical situations, only one of the optimizations is constrained

  • The numerator is always larger than the denominator (only equal to 1 if ^θ0=^θθ^0=θ^)

  • The smaller the ratio, the larger the evidence in favor of the null

  • The larger the ratio, the larger the evidence against the null

The likelihood ratio test (LRT) is any test that has a rejection region of the form : {x:λ(x)≥c}{x:λ(x)≥c} where cc is any number satisfying c>1c>1.

Monotone likelihood ratio

To generalize the optimality result of Neyman and Pearson to non-simple hypotheses, we need to introduce the following:

Def: A family of pdfs {f(x∣θ);θ∈Θ}{f(x∣θ);θ∈Θ} has monotone likelihood ratio if there exists a function T(x)T(x) such that whenever θ2>θ1θ2>θ1, the ratio f(t∣θ2)f(t∣θ1)f(t∣θ2)f(t∣θ1) is a nondecreasing function of t=T(x)t=T(x) on x={t:f(t∣θ1)>0orf(t∣θ2)>0}.x={t:f(t∣θ1)>0orf(t∣θ2)>0}. Further, the distribution is identifiable, that is g(t;θ1)≠g(t;θ2)g(t;θ1)≠g(t;θ2) whenever θ1≠θ2θ1≠θ2

Example The exponential family f(x∣θ)=exp{w(θ)t(x)−b(θ)}h(x)f(x∣θ)=exp⁡{w(θ)t(x)−b(θ)}h(x) if w(θ)w(θ) strictly increasing has likelihood ratio monotone in T=t(x)T=t(x).

UMP tests, one-sided tests

Consider the one-sided composite test

H0:θ≤θ0vsH1:θ>θ0H0:θ≤θ0vsH1:θ>θ0

Suppose that XX has density f(x∣θ)f(x∣θ) with monotone likelihood ratio in T(x)T(x). Then:

  1. The test ϕ∗ϕ∗ defined by ϕ∗(x)=⎧⎪⎨⎪⎩1T(x)>cγT(x)=c0T(x)<cϕ∗(x)={1T(x)>cγT(x)=c0T(x)<c is a uniformly most powerful (UMP) level αα test where α=Eθ0ϕ∗α=Eθ0ϕ∗.

    The constants cc and γγ can be adjusted to attain the desired significance level.

  2. The power function of ϕ∗ϕ∗ is nondecreasing, strictly increasing if 0<β(θ)<10<β(θ)<1

Proof - Power is nondecreasing

Suppose θ1<θ2θ1<θ2 and set L(x)=f(x∣θ2)f(x∣θ1)=g(T(x)∣θ2)g(T(x)∣θ1)L(x)=f(x∣θ2)f(x∣θ1)=g(T(x)∣θ2)g(T(x)∣θ1) By assumption, LL is a nondecreasing function of T(x)T(x). Let kk be such that (T=c)↦(L=k)(T=c)↦(L=k). Then ϕ∗(x)={1L>k0L<kϕ∗(x)={1L>k0L<k is a likelihood ratio test for the simple hypothesis H0:θ=θ1H0:θ=θ1 vs H1:θ=θ2H1:θ=θ2. By the corollary to the N-P Lemma, this test is most powerful and thus (see Corollary 12.4. (Keener 2010)) Eθ1ϕ∗≤Eθ2ϕ∗Eθ1ϕ∗≤Eθ2ϕ∗ (with strict inequality unless both expetations are either 0 or 1).

So, ϕ∗ϕ∗ has level supθ≤θ0Eθϕ∗(X)=Eθ0ϕ∗(X)=αsupθ≤θ0Eθϕ∗(X)=Eθ0ϕ∗(X)=α and nondecreasing power as stated in 2.

Proof - ϕ∗ϕ∗ is UMP

It remains to show that any test ~ϕϕ~ such that Eθ0~ϕ(X)≤αEθ0ϕ~(X)≤α has lower power than ϕ∗ϕ∗.

Take an arbitrary θ1>θ0θ1>θ0. From the NP Lemma, ϕ∗ϕ∗ is the most powerful test for the simple hypothesis H0:θ=θ0H0:θ=θ0 against the alternative H1:θ=θ1H1:θ=θ1, thus implying Eθ1ϕ∗≥Eθ1~ϕEθ1ϕ∗≥Eθ1ϕ~

Proof - Choice of cc and γγ

Finally, we show that cc nd γγ can be chosen so that Eθ0ϕ∗=αEθ0ϕ∗=α. Let G0G0 be the CDF of T(X)T(X) when θ=θ0θ=θ0. Let c=sup{t:G0(t)≤1−α}c=sup{t:G0(t)≤1−α}.

  • If t=T(x)>ct=T(x)>c, then G0(t)>1−αG0(t)>1−α.
  • G0G0 is right continuous, so G0(c)=limt↓cG0(t)≥1−αG0(c)=limt↓cG0(t)≥1−α
  • For t<ct<c, G0(t)≤1−αG0(t)≤1−α and G0(c−)=limt↑cG0(t)≤1−αG0(c−)=limt↑cG0(t)≤1−α
  • Define γ=G0(c)−(1−α)G0(c)−G0(c−)=G0(c)−(1−α)Pθ0(T=c)γ=G0(c)−(1−α)G0(c)−G0(c−)=G0(c)−(1−α)Pθ0(T=c) (note that if G0(c)−G0(c−)=0G0(c)−G0(c−)=0, then γγ need not to be defined).
  • Finally, Eθ0ϕ∗=γPθ0(T=c)+1⋅Pθ0(T>c)=G0(c)−(1−α)+(1−G0(c))=αEθ0ϕ∗=γPθ0(T=c)+1⋅Pθ0(T>c)=G0(c)−(1−α)+(1−G0(c))=α

Two-sided hypotheses

  • Suppose we define a test with R1(X)={T(X)≤t0}R1(X)={T(X)≤t0} α=P(¯X≤t0∣θ0)=P(¯X−θ0σ/√n≤t0−θ0σ/√n)α=P(X¯≤t0∣θ0)=P(X¯−θ0σ/n≤t0−θ0σ/n) Then, t0−θ0σ/√n=zα⇒t0=θ0+zασ√n=θ0−z1−ασ√nt0−θ0σ/n=zα⇒t0=θ0+zασn=θ0−z1−ασn

And, by Karlin-Rubin, test is UMP for H0:θ=θ0vsH1:θ<θ0.H0:θ=θ0vsH1:θ<θ0.

  • Repeat the same reasoning with R2(X)={T(X)≥t0}R2(X)={T(X)≥t0}, we get α=P(¯X≥t0∣θ0)=P(¯X−θ0σ/√n≥t0−θ0σ/√n)α=P(X¯≥t0∣θ0)=P(X¯−θ0σ/n≥t0−θ0σ/n) Now t0=θ0+z1−ασ√n=θ0−zασ√nt0=θ0+z1−ασn=θ0−zασn is UMP for H0:θ=θ0vsH1:θ>θ0.H0:θ=θ0vsH1:θ>θ0.

Two-sided hypotheses (continued)

  • The power of the test based on R1R1 is β1(θ)=Pθ(¯X<θ0−z1−ασ√n)=Pθ(¯X−θσ/√n<θ0−θσ/√n−z1−α)β1(θ)=Pθ(X¯<θ0−z1−ασn)=Pθ(X¯−θσ/n<θ0−θσ/n−z1−α)

  • The power of the test based on R2R2 is β2(θ)=Pθ(¯X>θ0+z1−ασ√n)=Pθ(¯X−θσ/√n>θ0−θσ/√n+z1−α)β2(θ)=Pθ(X¯>θ0+z1−ασn)=Pθ(X¯−θσ/n>θ0−θσ/n+z1−α)

  • Let θ′>θ0θ′>θ0, then β1(θ′)=Pθ′(¯X−θ′σ/√n≤θ0−θ′σ/√n−z1−α)=Φ(θ0−θ′σ/√n−z1−α)<Φ(−z1−α)=1−Φ(z1−α)<1−Φ(θ0−θ′σ/√n+z1−α)=β2(θ′)β1(θ′)=Pθ′(X¯−θ′σ/n≤θ0−θ′σ/n−z1−α)=Φ(θ0−θ′σ/n−z1−α)<Φ(−z1−α)=1−Φ(z1−α)<1−Φ(θ0−θ′σ/n+z1−α)=β2(θ′)

  • Similarly, for θ′<θ0θ′<θ0, β2(θ′)<β1(θ′)β2(θ′)<β1(θ′), so there is no UMP

Unbiased tests

  • The reason why in many cases we can’t find the UMP test is that the class CC of level-αα tests is too large.

  • We can compromise by restricting the class of tests we consider to compare the power functions in the a subclass of unbiased test statistics

Def: A test for the hypothesis θ∈Θ0vsθ∈Θc0θ∈Θ0vsθ∈Θ0c with power function β(θ)β(θ) is called if β(θ′)≥β(θ′′)for all θ′∈Θc0 and θ′′∈Θ0β(θ′)≥β(θ″)for all θ′∈Θ0c and θ″∈Θ0

  • An unbiased test must reject a false hypothesis (any θ′∈Θc0θ′∈Θ0c) with a probability larger than the probability to reject a true hypothesis (θ′′∈Θ0θ″∈Θ0).

  • If α=supθ∈Θ0β(θ)α=supθ∈Θ0β(θ) is the size of the unbiased test, then β(θ)≥α,for all θ∈Θc0.β(θ)≥α,for all θ∈Θ0c.

  • A test that is uniformly most powerful among all umbiased tests is called an UMPU test

UMPU tests

  • UMPU tests for the two-sided hypothesis H0:θ=θ0vsH:1θ≠θ0H0:θ=θ0vsH:1θ≠θ0 exist for exponential families.

Let α∈(0,1)α∈(0,1) and θ0∈int(Θ)θ0∈int(Θ). If XX has a distribution of the form f(x∣θ)=c(θ)h(x)exp{t(x)w(θ)}f(x∣θ)=c(θ)h(x)exp⁡{t(x)w(θ)} with ∞>w′(θ0)>0∞>w′(θ0)>0. Then there exists a uniformly most powerful unbiased test ϕ∗ϕ∗ of level αα, satisfying β′(θ0)=0β′(θ0)=0

  • ϕ∗ϕ∗ is a two-sided test: namely takes the form ϕ={1(T<tl)∪(T>tu)0tl<T<tuϕ={1(T<tl)∪(T>tu)0tl<T<tu and min{Eϕ1{T≤tl},Eϕ1{T≥tu}}>0min{Eϕ1{T≤tl},Eϕ1{T≥tu}}>0.

CmCm tests

The previous theorem comes as a corollary case of the following more general result:

  • Let ϕ+ϕ+ and ϕ−ϕ− be thw UMP tests for the one-sided alternatives θ>θ0θ>θ0 and θ<θ0θ<θ0 with powers β+(θ)β+(θ) and β−(θ)β−(θ).

  • From UMP property of ϕ+ϕ+ and ϕ−ϕ− it is easy to see that for any test ϕϕ, m−=:β′−(θ0)≤β′ϕ(θ0)≤limε↓0β+(θ0+ε)−β+(θ0)ε=β′+(θ0):=m+m−=:β−′(θ0)≤βϕ′(θ0)≤limε↓0β+(θ0+ε)−β+(θ0)ε=β+′(θ0):=m+

  • Now, denote CmCm the class of level αα tests such that β′ϕ(θ0)=mβϕ′(θ0)=m

  • Note that unbiased tests must have m=0m=0: suppose for example m<0m<0, then β′ϕ(θ0)=mβϕ′(θ0)=m and βϕβϕ is decreasing at θ0θ0. Thus, for θ′>θ0θ′>θ0, we get βϕ(θ′)<βϕ(θ0)βϕ(θ′)<βϕ(θ0), that contradicts unbiasedness, because θ′∈Θ1θ′∈Θ1

UMP tests in CmCm

Let α∈(0,1)α∈(0,1) and θ0∈int(Θ)θ0∈int(Θ). If XX has a distribution of the form f(x∣θ)=c(θ)h(x)exp{t(x)w(θ)}f(x∣θ)=c(θ)h(x)exp⁡{t(x)w(θ)} with ∞>w′(θ0)>0∞>w′(θ0)>0. If m∈(m−,m+)m∈(m−,m+) there exists a UMP level αα test in CmCm, say ϕ∗ϕ∗.

ϕ∗ϕ∗ is a two-sided test.

  • It is easy to see that (th, 12.20 Keener (2010)), for the exponential family as above: β′ϕ(θ)=w′(θ)Eθ(Tϕ)+d(logc(θ))Eθϕβϕ′(θ)=w′(θ)Eθ(Tϕ)+d(log⁡c(θ))Eθϕ

Example

Let X∼exponential(θ)X∼exponential(θ): f(x∣θ)=θe−θxf(x∣θ)=θe−θx for x>0x>0.

Consider testing H0:θ=1H0:θ=1 vs H1:θ≠1H1:θ≠1 based on one observation only.

The UMPU test then has the form: ϕ={1(X<tl)∪(X>tu)0tl<X<tuϕ={1(X<tl)∪(X>tu)0tl<X<tu where tu,tltu,tl must satisfy E1ϕ=1−∫tutle−xdx=1−e−tl−e−tu=αE1ϕ=1−∫tltue−xdx=1−e−tl−e−tu=α with the constraint β′ϕ(θ0)=0βϕ′(θ0)=0, that implies 0=w′(1)E1ϕX+d(log(c(1))E1ϕ=1−∫tutlxe−xdx+αE1ϕ0=w′(1)E1ϕX+d(log⁡(c(1))E1ϕ=1−∫tltuxe−xdx+E1ϕ⏞α Putting the two equations together: tle−tl=−(e−tl−1+α)log(e−tl−1+α)tle−tl=−(e−tl−1+α)log⁡(e−tl−1+α) The solution has to be found numerically.

(Cont.) Solving for P(X≤tl)=P(X>tu)=α/2P(X≤tl)=P(X>tu)=α/2 simplifies the computation, but in this case the test is biased, because m(θ0)=β′ϕ(θ0)≠0m(θ0)=βϕ′(θ0)≠0

0.0010.1000.050.0010.01090.02080.03070.04060.05050.06040.07030.08020.09010.1

Duality of tests and confidence sets

Tests and confidence intervals a have a one-2-one correspondence:

Recall that a random set S(X)S(X) is a 1−α1−α confidence region for a parameter θθ if Pθ(θ∈S(X))≥1−α,∀θ∈Θ.Pθ(θ∈S(X))≥1−α,∀θ∈Θ.

  • Let ϕϕ be a level αα binary test with acceptance region A(θ0)A(θ0) for the hypothesis θ=θ0θ=θ0.

Then the region S(X)={θ:X∈A(θ)}S(X)={θ:X∈A(θ)} is a 1−α1−α confidence region. In fact: Pθ(θ∈S(X))=Pθ(X∈A(θ))≥1−αPθ(θ∈S(X))=Pθ(X∈A(θ))≥1−α

  • Conversely, given the 1−α1−α confidence region SS, the test ϕ(X)={1θ0∈S(X)c0θ0∈S(X)ϕ(X)={1θ0∈S(X)c0θ0∈S(X) is a level αα test for θ=θ0θ=θ0 against θ≠θ0θ≠θ0.

Duality of tests and confidence sets

  • Optimality of tests should correspond to optimal confidence interval (in some sense).

  • Consider a confidence set S(X)S(X) defined as the inverse of a binary two-sided test ϕϕ (we assume for convenience Θ=(θl,θu)Θ=(θl,θu) with max(|θl|,|θu|)=∞max(|θl|,|θu|)=∞ allowed).

  • Let λ(A)λ(A) be the Lebesgue measure of the set AA. The average length of the confidence interval SS based on the test ϕϕ is: Eθλ(S(X)∖{θ})=Eθ∫Θ∖{θ}1{θ0∈S(X)}dθ0=∫X∫Θ∖{θ}1{θ0∈S(X)}dθ0dPθ(x)=∫Θ∖{θ}Pθ(θ0∈S(X))dθ0=∫Θ∖{θ}Pθ(X∈A(θ0))dθ0=∫Θ∖{θ}(1−β(θ0))dθ0Eθλ(S(X)∖{θ})=Eθ∫Θ∖{θ}1{θ0∈S(X)}dθ0=∫X∫Θ∖{θ}1{θ0∈S(X)}dθ0dPθ(x)=∫Θ∖{θ}Pθ(θ0∈S(X))dθ0=∫Θ∖{θ}Pθ(X∈A(θ0))dθ0=∫Θ∖{θ}(1−β(θ0))dθ0 Since a UMP(U) test ϕ∗ϕ∗ maximizes β∗(θ0)β∗(θ0) for all θ0≠θθ0≠θ, the mean length of the corresponding confidence set S∗S∗ is Eθλ(S(X)∖{θ})≥Eθλ(S∗(X)∖{θ})Eθλ(S(X)∖{θ})≥Eθλ(S∗(X)∖{θ})

Example

Let X∼exponential(λ)X∼exponential(λ) (with λ=EXλ=EX). Suppose we want a confidence interval for λλ.

We can obtain it by inverting a LR test for H0:λ=λ0vsH1:λ≠λ0H0:λ=λ0vsH1:λ≠λ0

Le LRT statistic is: λ(x)=λ−10exp{−∑ixi/λ0}supλ>0λ−1exp{−∑ixi/λ}=λ−10exp{−∑ixi/λ0}¯x−1exp{−∑ixi/¯x}=(¯xλ0)ne−n¯xλ0+nλ(x)=λ0−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/λ0}supλ>0λ−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/λ}=λ0−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/λ0}x¯−1exp⁡{−∑ixi/x¯}=(x¯λ0)ne−nx¯λ0+n

Rejection region is for small values of λ(x)λ(x)

So, acceptance region is A(λ0)={x:(¯xλ0)ne−n(¯x/λ0−1)≥k}A(λ0)={x:(x¯λ0)ne−n(x¯/λ0−1)≥k} where kk is defined implicitly by Pλ0(X∉A(λ0))=α.Pλ0(X∉A(λ0))=α.

Example (cont.)

Then, we obtain the confidence interval: CI(x)={λ:(¯xλ)ne−n(¯x/λ−1)≥k}={λ:(¯xλ)ne−n¯x/λ≥ke−n}CI(x)={λ:(x¯λ)ne−n(x¯/λ−1)≥k}={λ:(x¯λ)ne−nx¯/λ≥ke−n}

Pivotal Random Variables

Def.: A random variable Q(X,θ)Q(X,θ) is a pivotal quantity (or pivot) if its distribution does not depend on the parameter θθ. Here XX is the random sample.

  • If the pivotal quantity can be inverted as a function of θθ for each value of XX , then we may find a confidence region (interval) for θθ.
  • Inversion means that, for each xx and each subset AA of the range of Q(x,θ)Q(x,θ), there exists a subset R(x,A)R(x,A) of ΘΘ such that Q(x,θ)∈A⟺θ∈R(x,A)Q(x,θ)∈A⟺θ∈R(x,A) This implies that P(Q(X,θ)∈A)=P(θ∈R(X,A))P(Q(X,θ)∈A)=P(θ∈R(X,A))
  • From a (nontrivial) pivotal random variable one can often obtain a confidence interval
  • In general there may be more than one pivotal random variable and confidence intervals

Minimum lenght confidence intervals

An optimality theorem for confidence intervals is available for unimodal distributions.

Assume T=T(X)T=T(X) is a pivotal statistic for θθ and that its distribution f(t)f(t) is unimodal. If in the interval [a,b][a,b] satisfies

  1. ∫baf(t)dt=1−α∫abf(t)dt=1−α

  2. f(a)=f(b)>0f(a)=f(b)>0

  3. a≤t⋆≤ba≤t⋆≤b where t⋆t⋆ is a mode for f(t)f(t)

Then [a,b][a,b] is the shortest among all intervals that satisfies ∫baf(t)dt=1−α∫abf(t)dt=1−α.

Example (cont.)

10
00.0499000.02500.0050.010.0150.020.0250.030.0350.040.0450.0499

Fisher vs Neyman

  • The approach outlined so far follows Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework: the problem of testing is treated as a decision problem.

  • Neyman-Pearson wanted to find a mathematical justification to the significance tests of the Fisher school.

  • Even so, Fisher was quite adamant in opposing their approach.

  • Fisher refused the idea of the optimality as a property of a procedure, and claimed the central role of the experience of the research in making an informed decision conditional on the problem
  • Therefore, maximization of power is not relevant. Fisher is not very much interested in the alternative: a large value of the test statistic gives evidence against the null, because it makes less plausible the null hypothesis (low probability to observe that value under the null)

Fisher vs Neyman

  • The approach outlined so far follows Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework: the problem of testing is treated as a decision problem.

  • Neyman-Pearson wanted to find a mathematical justification to the significance tests of the Fisher school.

  • Even so, Fisher was quite adamant in opposing their approach.

  • While for N-P, the choice based on optimality is pre-experimental, for Fisher every conclusion (significance level) must be conditional on an available distribution constant. N-P principle is coherent with the idea of repetibility of the experiment
  • Sometimes the contrast is also referred as c contrast between p-values (or significance) and pre-determined αα level.

P-variables and p-values

Let us consider the null hypothesis Θ0Θ0.

Def: A p−p−variable for Θ0Θ0 is a [0,1]−[0,1]−valued random variable UU such that, for every α∈(0,1)α∈(0,1), and for all θ∈Θ0θ∈Θ0: Pθ(U≤α)≤αPθ(U≤α)≤α The p−p−variable is exact if Pθ(U≤α)=αPθ(U≤α)=α.

Def: A p−p−value is the value taken by the p−p−variable on observing the data.

A test that rejects H0:θ∈Θ0H0:θ∈Θ0 if and only if U=U(X)≤αU=U(X)≤α is a level αα test by construction.

A test result can be reported via its p−p−value rather than the acceptance/rejection decision. This clearly gives more information, because it reports the result of the test on a continuous scale.

P-variables and p-values

Let ϕϕ be a one-sided test with rejection region R(θ)={T(X)≥k}R(θ)={T(X)≥k}. Define for each sample point xx, the function p(x)=supθ∈Θ0Pθ(T(X)≥T(x))p(x)=supθ∈Θ0Pθ(T(X)≥T(x)) Then, the r.v. p(X)p(X) is a p−p−variable.

Proof. For any θ0∈Θ0θ0∈Θ0 pθ0(x)=Pθ0(T(X)≥T(x))=Pθ0(−T(X)≤−T(x))=Fθ0(−T(x))pθ0(x)=Pθ0(T(X)≥T(x))=Pθ0(−T(X)≤−T(x))=Fθ0(−T(x)) where Fθ0Fθ0 is the CDF of −T(X)−T(X) under θ0θ0. Then, by the probability integral transform, Fθ0(−T(X))d=pθ0(X)∼U(0,1)Fθ0(−T(X))=dpθ0(X)∼U(0,1).

Thus, for any α∈(0,1)α∈(0,1), Pθ(p(X)≤α)=Pθ(supθpθ(X)≤α)≤Pθ(pθ(X)≤α)=αPθ(p(X)≤α)=Pθ(supθpθ(X)≤α)≤Pθ(pθ(X)≤α)=α

References:

Casella, G., and R. Berger. 2024. Statistical Inference. Chapman & Hall.

Keener, Robert W. 2010. Theoretical Statistics. Topics for a Core Course. Springer.

Lehmann, E. L. 1993. “The Fisher, Neyman-Pearson Theories of Testing Hypotheses: One Theory or Two?” Journal of the American Statistical Association 88 (424): 1242–49.

Lehmann, E. L., and Joseph P. Romano. 2022. Testing Statistical Hypotheses. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-70578-7.

Pace, L., and A. Salvan. 1997. Principles of Statistical Inference from a Neo-Fisherian Perspective. World Scientific.
